Thanks for watching!

item.title: The GTA 6 Landing Page Scroll Animation That Broke The Internet
item.itunes:title: The GTA 6 Landing Page Scroll Animation That Broke The Internet
item.itunes:author: Codegrid
item.itunes:summary:
影片摘要


影片的開頭，講者收到了許多觀眾的要求，希望示範如何重現GTA 6網站上令人驚豔的捲動動畫。他表示，這個效果非常吸引人，也因此會是個有趣的挑戰。這個動畫的特色是，當使用者向下捲動時，Logo和內容會平滑地消失，同時英雄圖片會向外縮放。接著，Logo會以類似挖空視窗的方式逐漸顯現，並透過其形狀揭示背景。再向下捲動，文字會以平滑的漸變效果出現。講者承認，重建這個效果比預期的複雜，經過七個小時的努力和四種不同的嘗試後，他成功地利用SVG、GSAP和Scroll Trigger重現了這個動畫效果。
他採用了SVG遮罩的方法來實現揭示效果。他建立了一個SVG遮罩，其中包含定義Logo形狀的路徑，然後將此遮罩應用到覆蓋視窗的矩形上，創造了一個Logo形狀的「窗口」，這個窗口會顯示下方的內容，並隱藏其他所有內容。影片將逐步示範如何重現相同的效果。
接著，講者提到了提供源碼的贊助方案，並鼓勵觀眾點讚和訂閱。

素材準備


首先，講者說明了專案所需的素材：英雄區塊的背景圖片、前景圖片（人物角色分離在透明背景上）、Logo的PNG版本、以及Logo的SVG版本。SVG版本尤其重要，需要從設計工具匯出，並提取路徑資料。為了方便操作，他建立了一個名為logo.js的獨立檔案，將這個路徑資料匯出為名為logoData的字串變數。這個logoData將被用於建立遮罩，透過Logo形狀來顯示背景。

HTML結構


接下來，講者概述了HTML結構。主要分為兩個部分：英雄區塊，包含動畫；以及在捲動後出現的後續區塊。
英雄區塊中，首先加入一個具有hero image container class的div，用於容納分層的圖片。在這個容器中，背景圖片作為第一個元素。接下來，加入一個具有hero image logo class的div，其中包含Logo圖片。這個Logo初始可見，但會在捲動時淡出。第三個元素是前景圖片，也就是分離的人物角色圖片。在這些圖片下方，增加一個具有hero image copy class的div，包含一段文字。
在圖片容器之外，增加一個fade overlay div，用於創建淡入效果。關鍵的元素是具有overlay class的div，其中包含SVG。在這個SVG中，定義了一個名為logo re mask的遮罩，包含一個全螢幕的矩形和一個具有ID為logo mask的路徑元素。這個路徑將使用JavaScript中的logoData填充。然後，將這個遮罩應用於填充深色的全螢幕矩形上，創造遮罩效果。
此外，還有一個空的logo container div，用於定位Logo遮罩。接著是包含標題文字的overlay copy div，文字會在捲動序列中稍後出現。最後，外部區塊將包含一些佔位符文字，在捲動完動畫後可見。

CSS樣式設定


接下來，講者開始樣式設定。首先，他導入了Google Fonts的DMsense字體。然後，重置所有預設的瀏覽器邊距和填充，並確保使用border box的盒模型。
接著，設定body的字體、深色背景和白色文字。他隱藏了水平溢出，以避免不必要的滾動條。他將所有圖片設定為佔據容器的100%寬度和高度，並使用object fit cover來保持長寬比。
對於排版，他將H1元素設定為大寫，使用大字體和緊密的行距，以及稍微負值的字母間距，營造現代感。段落也被設定為大寫，但字體較小且使用中等粗細的字重。
每個區塊都會佔據整個視窗的寬度和高度，並使用相對定位，以便對其中的元素進行絕對定位。背景顏色與body顏色相同，並且隱藏英雄圖片容器及其樣式圖片的溢出，以及fade overlay。他將它們進行絕對定位，以填充整個容器。
Logo圖片進行特殊處理，從頂部定位20%，並水平居中。他設定了特定的寬度，並允許高度自動調整，同時保持長寬比。捲動提示文字位於螢幕底部並居中。
fade overlay具有白色背景，會在捲動時逐漸改變不透明度。包含SVG遮罩的overlay被絕對定位，高度為200%，並使用Z軸索引1，確保其位於圖片之上。設定了transform origin為水平中心和垂直15%，用於控制縮放。
對於logo container，定位為從頂部算起25%，並水平居中，這決定了Logo遮罩出現的位置。他提到，這個值需要根據不同的Logo進行調整，並解釋了他的Logo中眼睛的設計。
包含文字的overlay copy位於視窗底部附近，並使用Z軸索引2，確保其出現在其他元素之上。對於此文字，使用了background clip技術來建立文字遮罩，以便稍後在JavaScript中顯示漸變背景。Transform origin設定為中心上方，控制動畫期間的縮放。
[音樂]
後續區塊的文字僅使用flex佈局水平和垂直居中。最後，針對較小的螢幕添加了一些響應式調整，縮小字體，並確保overlay copy在行動裝置上佔據全部寬度。

JavaScript程式碼


講者開始講解JavaScript，使頁面動起來。首先，他導入了必要的依賴項，包括logo.js中的SVG路徑資料、用於動畫的GSAP、用於觸發基於滾動位置動畫的GSAP的Scroll Trigger插件、以及用於平滑滾動體驗的Lenis。
導入後，他將Scroll Trigger插件註冊到GSAP，以便使用。然後，他將所有內容包裝在DOM content loaded事件監聽器中，確保程式碼在頁面完全載入後運行。
在事件監聽器中，他設定了Lenis平滑滾動，並讓Lenis在發生滾動時更新Scroll Trigger。他使用了現成的程式碼。
接著，他選取了所有DOM元素，包括背景圖片容器、起始Logo、捲動提示文字、白色overlay、包含遮罩的SVG overlay、以及標題文字。這些選取用於操作這些元素，使其隨著使用者捲動而改變。
接下來，他設定Logo遮罩及其尺寸。他定義了overlay的初始縮放值為350，這個數值決定了遮罩開始時的縮放程度，這個數值可以根據Logo類型進行調整。他選取了在HTML中定義的logo container，以及SVG中的logo mask元素。
接著是一個重要的步驟，他將SVG路徑的D屬性設定為使用匯入的logoData，這定義了SVG遮罩中Logo的實際形狀。
為了正確定位Logo，需要確定容器和SVG路徑的大小和位置。他使用get bounding client rect函式獲取容器的確切位置和尺寸，以及get bounding box方法獲取Logo形狀的尺寸。然後，他計算縮放因子。因為SVG Logo路徑需要正確地適合容器。他將容器的寬度除以路徑的寬度，得到水平縮放比例，並將容器的高度除以路徑的高度，得到垂直縮放比例。
================================================

In the last few days, a bunch of folks. reached out requesting a video on this. stunning scroll animation from the GTA 6. website. When I first saw it, even I was. instantly captivated and knew it would. be an exciting challenge to recreate. this effect from scratch. As you can. see, when scrolling down, the logo and. the content of this section smoothly. fade away while the hero image zooms. out. Then, the logo cinematically starts. coming into view as a cutout window. while still revealing the background. through its shape. As you scroll more,. some text emerges with a smooth gradient. fade. I'm not going to lie, when I. started trying to rebuild this, it. turned out way more complex than I. expected. But after about 7 hours and. trying out like four different. approaches, I was finally able to. replicate the scroll animation using. SVGs, GSAP, and scroll trigger. The. technique I landed on uses an SVG. masking approach for the reveal effect. Basically, I created an SVG mask with a. path inside it that defines the logo. shape and then applied this mask to a. rectangle covering the view port that. created sort of a window in the shape of. the logo that reveals what's underneath. while hiding everything else. In this. video, I'll show you step by step how to. recreate the same effect yourself. If. you find these videos helpful, a like. and subscribe would really mean a lot. Also, if you want to access the source. code for this project, plus hundreds of. other similar micro projects along with. the complete website template every. month, you can check out the pro. membership via the link in the. description. All right, let's dive in. Before we dive into coding, let's go. through the assets we'll need for this. project. First, we need a background. image for hero section. I'm using this. vibrant GTA themed artwork from 4K. wallpapers.com, but you can substitute. any image you prefer. Next, we need a. foreground image with just the. characters isolated on a transparent. background. This is crucial for our. layering effect. It allows us to place. the logo behind the characters, but in. front of the background. We'll stack. these elements with the background at. the bottom, logo in the middle, and.

character image on the top. Next, for. the logo, I have created the simple PNG. version in Figma. You can use any logo. design that works for your project. Most. importantly, you'll also need an SVG. version of your logo. After exporting. from your design tool, open the SVG in. any browser and extract the path data. from it. This path information is. essential for creating our mask effect. To make this easier, I've created a. separate file called logo.js that. exports this path data as a string. variable named logo data. We'll import. this into our main script and use it to. create the mask that reles our. background through the logo shape. With. these assets prepared, we are ready to. start building our HTML structure. We'll need two main sections. A hero. section which will contain our animation. and an outro section that appears after. scrolling. Inside the hero section, I'm. first adding a div with the class hero. image container that would hold our. layered images. Within this container,. I'm placing the background image as the. first element. This will be our main. backdrop. Next, I'm adding another div. with the class name hero image logo. which contains our logo image. This logo. will initially be visible but will fade. out as we scroll. Then I'm adding our. second layer image. This is isolated. character image that will appear on top. of everything else. Below that I'm. adding a div with the class hero image. copy containing a paragraph text. Now. outside the image container I'm adding a. fade overlay div which will help create. a fade effect as we scroll. The next. element is crucial a div with the class. name overlay containing an SVG. This is. where the magic happens. Inside the SVG,. I have defined a mask called logo re. mask which contains a full screen. rectangle and a path element with the ID. logo mask. This path will be populated. with our logo data from JavaScript. Then. I'm applying this mask to a full screen. rectangle filled with a dark color. This. creates our mask effect where the logo. shape will reveal the content. underneath. I'm also adding an empty. logo container div which will be used as. a reference point for positioning our. logo mask. Then there is an overlay copy. div containing an H1 with a text that.

will appear later in the scroll. sequence. Finally, the outer section. will have some placeholder text that. will be visible after scrolling past the. animation. That would be it for the HTML. structure. Let's get to styling. now. First, you can see I've imported. the DMsense font from Google Fonts. Now. let's start by resetting all default. browser margins, paddings, and ensuring. box sizing is set to border box. For the. body, I'm setting our font family to. DMs, giving it a dark background color,. and white text. I'm also hiding any. horizontal overflow to prevent unwanted. scroll bars. I'm setting all images to. take up 100% of their containers width. and height with object fit cover to. maintain aspect ratios. For typography,. I'm styling the H1 elements to be. uppercase, giving a large font size with. tight line spacing and slightly negative. letter spacing for that modern. look. Paragraphs are also styled to be. uppercase but smaller and with medium. font. weight. Each section will take up the. full view port width and height with. relative positioning. So, we can. absolutely position elements inside. them. I'm setting the background to. match our body color and hiding any. overflow for our hero image container. and its styled images as well as the. fade overlay. I'm absolutely positioning. them to fill the entire. container. The logo image gets special. treatment. It's positioned 20% from the. top and centered horizontally. I am setting a specific width and. allowing the height to adjust. automatically while maintaining the. aspect. ratio. The scroll prompt text is. positioned at the bottom of the screen. and. centered. I'm adding will change opacity. to optimize the animation. performance. Our fade overlay is given a white. background and will gradually change. opacity during. scrolling. The overlay that contains our. SVG mask is positioned absolutely but. with 200% height and a Z index of one to. ensure it sits above our images. I am. setting its transform origin to the. center horizontally and 15% vertically.

which will control how it scales. Now. for the logo container, this is crucial. I'm positioning it fixed at 25% from the. top and centered horizontally. This. position is important because it. determines where your logo mask will. appear. You'll likely need to adjust. this value based on your specific logo. In my case, I wanted the zoom effect to. happen through the eye character present. in the last line in my logo. So, 25%. worked well. Different logos will. require different positioning. So, be. prepared to experiment with this value. The overlay copy that contains our text. is positioned near the bottom of the. viewport with a zindex of two to ensure. it appears above the other. elements. For this text, I'm using the. background clip technique to create a. text mask that will reveal a gradient. background later in our. JavaScript. I'm setting the transform. origin to the center top which will. control how it scales during animation. [Music]. The outro section text is simply. centered horizontally and vertically. using flex. layout. Finally, I'm adding some. responsive adjustments for smaller. screens, reducing font sizes, and. ensuring the overlay copy takes the full. width on mobile. devices. Let's get to JavaScript now and. bring this page to life. First, I'm importing the necessary. dependencies. The logo data from our. logojs file which contains the SVG path. data. Gap for handling our animations. Scroll trigger plug-in for GSAP which. will trigger animations based on scroll. position. Lenis, a smooth scrolling. library to make the experience more. polished. After importing these, I'm. registering the scroll trigger plug-in. with GSA so we can use it. Now I'm. wrapping everything in a DOM content. loaded event listener to make sure our. code only runs after the page has fully. loaded. Inside this event listener I'm. setting up Lennis source mode scrolling. will create a new Lennis instance and. tells it to update scroll trigger. whenever scrolling happens. You can find. this block of code on the documentation. website. I've used it as is without any. change.

Now I'm selecting all DOM elements. We'll need to animate the container with. our background images, the logo that. appears at the beginning, the scroll. down text, the white overlay that will. fade in, the SVG overlay that contains. our mask, the heading text that will. appear later in the animation. These. selections give us references to. manipulate these elements as the user. scrolls. Next, I'm setting up the logo. mask and its dimensions. I start by. defining an initial scale value of 350. for our overlay. This is a really large. number that determines how zoomed in the. mask will be at the beginning. You can. play around this value based on your. logo type. I am selecting the logo. container we defined in our HTML and the. logo mask element from our SVG. Now. comes an important step. I'm setting the. D attribute of our SVG path to use the. logo data we imported. This is what. defines the actual shape of our logo in. the SVG mask. To position the logo. properly, I need to figure out both the. size and the position of our container. and the SVG path. For the container, I'm. using get bounding client rect function. which gives me its exact position and. dimensions on the page. For the SVG. path, I'm using get bounding box method. which tells me the dimensions of our. logo shape. Now I'm calculating scaling. factors. This is important because our. SVG logo path needs to properly fit. within our container. I'm dividing the container's width by. the path's width to get the horizontal. scale and the container's height by the. path height to get the vertical. scale. Then I'm taking the smaller one. to preserve the logo shape without. stretching it. The positioning. calculations help center our logo. I'm. starting with the container position,. then adding an offset to center the. logo, and finally adjusting for the. logo's internal coordinates. This ensures the logo appears exactly. where we want it in our. mask. Finally, I'm applying these. calculations to position and scale our. SVG path using the transform attribute. This tells the browser exactly where to. place our logo mask and how large it. should be. These precise calculations. are what allow our effect to work with.

any logo shape and size. Without them,. the mask wouldn't align properly and our. reveal effect wouldn't work correctly. Next, I'm creating our scroll trigger. instance. This is what will control all. of our effects as the user scrolls down. the page. I'll first set up the scroll. trigger with some important options. I'm. using hero section as the trigger. element, which means our animation will. start when this section enters the. viewport. I'm setting start to top. So,. the animation begins right away when the. top of our hero section reaches the top. of the viewport. For the end value, I'm. setting it to five times the viewport. height. This means our animation will. take place over a long scroll distance,. giving us plenty of room to create a. smooth gradual effect. I'm also enabling. pin, which keeps our hero section fixed. on screen while the animation plays, and. pin spacing, which maintains the scroll. height. The scrub value of one creates a. slight delay between scrolling and the. animation updating, making it feel. smoother. Next, I'm defining what. happens during the scroll in the. onupdate call back. This function. receives a self parameter that contains. information about the scroll progress. I'll start by getting the current scroll. progress which is a value between 0 and. 1 representing how far through the. animation we are. Then I'm calculating. the fade opacity for our initial. elements for the first 15% of scrolling. I want the logo and scroll text to. gradually fade out. I'm calculating this. with a formula that converts our. progress to an opacity value between 1. and zero. If this scroll progress is. below 15%, I'm setting the opacity of. these elements based on our calculation. Otherwise, I'm setting the opacity to. zero to keep them hidden. Next, for the. first 85% of scrolling, I'm handling the. scaling effects. First, I'm normalizing. the progress to a 01 range within this. portion of the scroll. I'm calculating. the scale of our hero image container,. starting at 1.5 and gradually reducing. to one as we scroll. This creates that. zoom out effect for the background. For. the overlay scale, I'm using a formula. that starts at our large initial overlay. scale value and gradually reduces it to. one. The power function helps create a.

smooth exponential transition instead of. a linear one. This is what creates the. effect of zooming out through the logo. shape. After 25% of scrolling, I start fading. in our white overlay. I'm calculating. its opacity to smoothly transition from. 0 to 1 between 25% and 65% of the. scroll. Finally, I'm applying these calculated. values using GAP's set method, which. updates the properties right away. Now,. for the final part of our scroll. animation, I'm adding the text reveal. effect that happens between 60% and 85%. of our scroll progress. When the scroll. progress is between 6 and 85, I want to. gradually reveal our heading text with a. smooth gradient effect. First, I'm. calculating a normalized progress value. just for this specific section of the. scroll. Converting it from a 0 to one. range. I'm setting a gradient spread of. 100, which determines how sharp or. gradual our text will be. Then, I'm. calculating the position of the. gradient's bottom edge, starting at 240. and moving upward as we scroll. The. gradient's top edge is positioned 100. pixels above the bottom edge. Then, I'm. scaling the text slightly as it appears,. starting from 1.25 25 times its normal. size and reducing it to one as you. scroll. This creates a subtle zoom. effect on the text itself. Now comes the. cool part. I am setting a linear. gradient as the background for our text. The gradient starts with a dark. background color at the top, then. transitions to a reddish color at the. bottom. The gradient positions are. dynamically calculated based on our. scroll progress. Because we set the text. to use background clip text in our CSS,. only the text itself shows this. gradient. As we scroll, the gradient. moves upward through the text, creating. that smooth rival effect from bottom to. top. I'm also gradually increasing the. opacity of the entire text element as we. scroll through this section. If the. scroll progress is less than 6, I'm. keeping the text completely invisible by. setting its opacity to zero. And with. that, our scroll trigger setup is. complete. This configuration creates the. entire animated sequence. Hope you found. the video helpful. See you in the next.

one.

=============

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lh5fQIlyOe0

Learn how to recreate GTA VI's mind-blowing scroll animation using HTML, CSS, JavaScript, GSAP and ScrollTrigger! This step-by-step tutorial shows you how to build that cinematic logo reveal effect that everyone's talking about.

Source Code: https://codegrid.gumroad.com/l/codegridpro/

Inspiration: Rockstar Games

Website: https://www.rockstargames.com/VI

Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/codegridweb/

Twitter: https://twitter.com/codegridweb/

Public Discord: https://discord.gg/B8B9MXxuSS

Music from Epidemic Sound.

Thanks for watching!
item.description:
影片摘要


影片的開頭，講者收到了許多觀眾的要求，希望示範如何重現GTA 6網站上令人驚豔的捲動動畫。他表示，這個效果非常吸引人，也因此會是個有趣的挑戰。這個動畫的特色是，當使用者向下捲動時，Logo和內容會平滑地消失，同時英雄圖片會向外縮放。接著，Logo會以類似挖空視窗的方式逐漸顯現，並透過其形狀揭示背景。再向下捲動，文字會以平滑的漸變效果出現。講者承認，重建這個效果比預期的複雜，經過七個小時的努力和四種不同的嘗試後，他成功地利用SVG、GSAP和Scroll Trigger重現了這個動畫效果。
他採用了SVG遮罩的方法來實現揭示效果。他建立了一個SVG遮罩，其中包含定義Logo形狀的路徑，然後將此遮罩應用到覆蓋視窗的矩形上，創造了一個Logo形狀的「窗口」，這個窗口會顯示下方的內容，並隱藏其他所有內容。影片將逐步示範如何重現相同的效果。
接著，講者提到了提供源碼的贊助方案，並鼓勵觀眾點讚和訂閱。

素材準備


首先，講者說明了專案所需的素材：英雄區塊的背景圖片、前景圖片（人物角色分離在透明背景上）、Logo的PNG版本、以及Logo的SVG版本。SVG版本尤其重要，需要從設計工具匯出，並提取路徑資料。為了方便操作，他建立了一個名為logo.js的獨立檔案，將這個路徑資料匯出為名為logoData的字串變數。這個logoData將被用於建立遮罩，透過Logo形狀來顯示背景。

HTML結構


接下來，講者概述了HTML結構。主要分為兩個部分：英雄區塊，包含動畫；以及在捲動後出現的後續區塊。
英雄區塊中，首先加入一個具有hero image container class的div，用於容納分層的圖片。在這個容器中，背景圖片作為第一個元素。接下來，加入一個具有hero image logo class的div，其中包含Logo圖片。這個Logo初始可見，但會在捲動時淡出。第三個元素是前景圖片，也就是分離的人物角色圖片。在這些圖片下方，增加一個具有hero image copy class的div，包含一段文字。
在圖片容器之外，增加一個fade overlay div，用於創建淡入效果。關鍵的元素是具有overlay class的div，其中包含SVG。在這個SVG中，定義了一個名為logo re mask的遮罩，包含一個全螢幕的矩形和一個具有ID為logo mask的路徑元素。這個路徑將使用JavaScript中的logoData填充。然後，將這個遮罩應用於填充深色的全螢幕矩形上，創造遮罩效果。
此外，還有一個空的logo container div，用於定位Logo遮罩。接著是包含標題文字的overlay copy div，文字會在捲動序列中稍後出現。最後，外部區塊將包含一些佔位符文字，在捲動完動畫後可見。

CSS樣式設定


接下來，講者開始樣式設定。首先，他導入了Google Fonts的DMsense字體。然後，重置所有預設的瀏覽器邊距和填充，並確保使用border box的盒模型。
接著，設定body的字體、深色背景和白色文字。他隱藏了水平溢出，以避免不必要的滾動條。他將所有圖片設定為佔據容器的100%寬度和高度，並使用object fit cover來保持長寬比。
對於排版，他將H1元素設定為大寫，使用大字體和緊密的行距，以及稍微負值的字母間距，營造現代感。段落也被設定為大寫，但字體較小且使用中等粗細的字重。
每個區塊都會佔據整個視窗的寬度和高度，並使用相對定位，以便對其中的元素進行絕對定位。背景顏色與body顏色相同，並且隱藏英雄圖片容器及其樣式圖片的溢出，以及fade overlay。他將它們進行絕對定位，以填充整個容器。
Logo圖片進行特殊處理，從頂部定位20%，並水平居中。他設定了特定的寬度，並允許高度自動調整，同時保持長寬比。捲動提示文字位於螢幕底部並居中。
fade overlay具有白色背景，會在捲動時逐漸改變不透明度。包含SVG遮罩的overlay被絕對定位，高度為200%，並使用Z軸索引1，確保其位於圖片之上。設定了transform origin為水平中心和垂直15%，用於控制縮放。
對於logo container，定位為從頂部算起25%，並水平居中，這決定了Logo遮罩出現的位置。他提到，這個值需要根據不同的Logo進行調整，並解釋了他的Logo中眼睛的設計。
包含文字的overlay copy位於視窗底部附近，並使用Z軸索引2，確保其出現在其他元素之上。對於此文字，使用了background clip技術來建立文字遮罩，以便稍後在JavaScript中顯示漸變背景。Transform origin設定為中心上方，控制動畫期間的縮放。
[音樂]
後續區塊的文字僅使用flex佈局水平和垂直居中。最後，針對較小的螢幕添加了一些響應式調整，縮小字體，並確保overlay copy在行動裝置上佔據全部寬度。

JavaScript程式碼


講者開始講解JavaScript，使頁面動起來。首先，他導入了必要的依賴項，包括logo.js中的SVG路徑資料、用於動畫的GSAP、用於觸發基於滾動位置動畫的GSAP的Scroll Trigger插件、以及用於平滑滾動體驗的Lenis。
導入後，他將Scroll Trigger插件註冊到GSAP，以便使用。然後，他將所有內容包裝在DOM content loaded事件監聽器中，確保程式碼在頁面完全載入後運行。
在事件監聽器中，他設定了Lenis平滑滾動，並讓Lenis在發生滾動時更新Scroll Trigger。他使用了現成的程式碼。
接著，他選取了所有DOM元素，包括背景圖片容器、起始Logo、捲動提示文字、白色overlay、包含遮罩的SVG overlay、以及標題文字。這些選取用於操作這些元素，使其隨著使用者捲動而改變。
接下來，他設定Logo遮罩及其尺寸。他定義了overlay的初始縮放值為350，這個數值決定了遮罩開始時的縮放程度，這個數值可以根據Logo類型進行調整。他選取了在HTML中定義的logo container，以及SVG中的logo mask元素。
接著是一個重要的步驟，他將SVG路徑的D屬性設定為使用匯入的logoData，這定義了SVG遮罩中Logo的實際形狀。
為了正確定位Logo，需要確定容器和SVG路徑的大小和位置。他使用get bounding client rect函式獲取容器的確切位置和尺寸，以及get bounding box方法獲取Logo形狀的尺寸。然後，他計算縮放因子。因為SVG Logo路徑需要正確地適合容器。他將容器的寬度除以路徑的寬度，得到水平縮放比例，並將容器的高度除以路徑的高度，得到垂直縮放比例。
================================================

In the last few days, a bunch of folks. reached out requesting a video on this. stunning scroll animation from the GTA 6. website. When I first saw it, even I was. instantly captivated and knew it would. be an exciting challenge to recreate. this effect from scratch. As you can. see, when scrolling down, the logo and. the content of this section smoothly. fade away while the hero image zooms. out. Then, the logo cinematically starts. coming into view as a cutout window. while still revealing the background. through its shape. As you scroll more,. some text emerges with a smooth gradient. fade. I'm not going to lie, when I. started trying to rebuild this, it. turned out way more complex than I. expected. But after about 7 hours and. trying out like four different. approaches, I was finally able to. replicate the scroll animation using. SVGs, GSAP, and scroll trigger. The. technique I landed on uses an SVG. masking approach for the reveal effect. Basically, I created an SVG mask with a. path inside it that defines the logo. shape and then applied this mask to a. rectangle covering the view port that. created sort of a window in the shape of. the logo that reveals what's underneath. while hiding everything else. In this. video, I'll show you step by step how to. recreate the same effect yourself. If. you find these videos helpful, a like. and subscribe would really mean a lot. Also, if you want to access the source. code for this project, plus hundreds of. other similar micro projects along with. the complete website template every. month, you can check out the pro. membership via the link in the. description. All right, let's dive in. Before we dive into coding, let's go. through the assets we'll need for this. project. First, we need a background. image for hero section. I'm using this. vibrant GTA themed artwork from 4K. wallpapers.com, but you can substitute. any image you prefer. Next, we need a. foreground image with just the. characters isolated on a transparent. background. This is crucial for our. layering effect. It allows us to place. the logo behind the characters, but in. front of the background. We'll stack. these elements with the background at. the bottom, logo in the middle, and.

character image on the top. Next, for. the logo, I have created the simple PNG. version in Figma. You can use any logo. design that works for your project. Most. importantly, you'll also need an SVG. version of your logo. After exporting. from your design tool, open the SVG in. any browser and extract the path data. from it. This path information is. essential for creating our mask effect. To make this easier, I've created a. separate file called logo.js that. exports this path data as a string. variable named logo data. We'll import. this into our main script and use it to. create the mask that reles our. background through the logo shape. With. these assets prepared, we are ready to. start building our HTML structure. We'll need two main sections. A hero. section which will contain our animation. and an outro section that appears after. scrolling. Inside the hero section, I'm. first adding a div with the class hero. image container that would hold our. layered images. Within this container,. I'm placing the background image as the. first element. This will be our main. backdrop. Next, I'm adding another div. with the class name hero image logo. which contains our logo image. This logo. will initially be visible but will fade. out as we scroll. Then I'm adding our. second layer image. This is isolated. character image that will appear on top. of everything else. Below that I'm. adding a div with the class hero image. copy containing a paragraph text. Now. outside the image container I'm adding a. fade overlay div which will help create. a fade effect as we scroll. The next. element is crucial a div with the class. name overlay containing an SVG. This is. where the magic happens. Inside the SVG,. I have defined a mask called logo re. mask which contains a full screen. rectangle and a path element with the ID. logo mask. This path will be populated. with our logo data from JavaScript. Then. I'm applying this mask to a full screen. rectangle filled with a dark color. This. creates our mask effect where the logo. shape will reveal the content. underneath. I'm also adding an empty. logo container div which will be used as. a reference point for positioning our. logo mask. Then there is an overlay copy. div containing an H1 with a text that.

will appear later in the scroll. sequence. Finally, the outer section. will have some placeholder text that. will be visible after scrolling past the. animation. That would be it for the HTML. structure. Let's get to styling. now. First, you can see I've imported. the DMsense font from Google Fonts. Now. let's start by resetting all default. browser margins, paddings, and ensuring. box sizing is set to border box. For the. body, I'm setting our font family to. DMs, giving it a dark background color,. and white text. I'm also hiding any. horizontal overflow to prevent unwanted. scroll bars. I'm setting all images to. take up 100% of their containers width. and height with object fit cover to. maintain aspect ratios. For typography,. I'm styling the H1 elements to be. uppercase, giving a large font size with. tight line spacing and slightly negative. letter spacing for that modern. look. Paragraphs are also styled to be. uppercase but smaller and with medium. font. weight. Each section will take up the. full view port width and height with. relative positioning. So, we can. absolutely position elements inside. them. I'm setting the background to. match our body color and hiding any. overflow for our hero image container. and its styled images as well as the. fade overlay. I'm absolutely positioning. them to fill the entire. container. The logo image gets special. treatment. It's positioned 20% from the. top and centered horizontally. I am setting a specific width and. allowing the height to adjust. automatically while maintaining the. aspect. ratio. The scroll prompt text is. positioned at the bottom of the screen. and. centered. I'm adding will change opacity. to optimize the animation. performance. Our fade overlay is given a white. background and will gradually change. opacity during. scrolling. The overlay that contains our. SVG mask is positioned absolutely but. with 200% height and a Z index of one to. ensure it sits above our images. I am. setting its transform origin to the. center horizontally and 15% vertically.

which will control how it scales. Now. for the logo container, this is crucial. I'm positioning it fixed at 25% from the. top and centered horizontally. This. position is important because it. determines where your logo mask will. appear. You'll likely need to adjust. this value based on your specific logo. In my case, I wanted the zoom effect to. happen through the eye character present. in the last line in my logo. So, 25%. worked well. Different logos will. require different positioning. So, be. prepared to experiment with this value. The overlay copy that contains our text. is positioned near the bottom of the. viewport with a zindex of two to ensure. it appears above the other. elements. For this text, I'm using the. background clip technique to create a. text mask that will reveal a gradient. background later in our. JavaScript. I'm setting the transform. origin to the center top which will. control how it scales during animation. [Music]. The outro section text is simply. centered horizontally and vertically. using flex. layout. Finally, I'm adding some. responsive adjustments for smaller. screens, reducing font sizes, and. ensuring the overlay copy takes the full. width on mobile. devices. Let's get to JavaScript now and. bring this page to life. First, I'm importing the necessary. dependencies. The logo data from our. logojs file which contains the SVG path. data. Gap for handling our animations. Scroll trigger plug-in for GSAP which. will trigger animations based on scroll. position. Lenis, a smooth scrolling. library to make the experience more. polished. After importing these, I'm. registering the scroll trigger plug-in. with GSA so we can use it. Now I'm. wrapping everything in a DOM content. loaded event listener to make sure our. code only runs after the page has fully. loaded. Inside this event listener I'm. setting up Lennis source mode scrolling. will create a new Lennis instance and. tells it to update scroll trigger. whenever scrolling happens. You can find. this block of code on the documentation. website. I've used it as is without any. change.

Now I'm selecting all DOM elements. We'll need to animate the container with. our background images, the logo that. appears at the beginning, the scroll. down text, the white overlay that will. fade in, the SVG overlay that contains. our mask, the heading text that will. appear later in the animation. These. selections give us references to. manipulate these elements as the user. scrolls. Next, I'm setting up the logo. mask and its dimensions. I start by. defining an initial scale value of 350. for our overlay. This is a really large. number that determines how zoomed in the. mask will be at the beginning. You can. play around this value based on your. logo type. I am selecting the logo. container we defined in our HTML and the. logo mask element from our SVG. Now. comes an important step. I'm setting the. D attribute of our SVG path to use the. logo data we imported. This is what. defines the actual shape of our logo in. the SVG mask. To position the logo. properly, I need to figure out both the. size and the position of our container. and the SVG path. For the container, I'm. using get bounding client rect function. which gives me its exact position and. dimensions on the page. For the SVG. path, I'm using get bounding box method. which tells me the dimensions of our. logo shape. Now I'm calculating scaling. factors. This is important because our. SVG logo path needs to properly fit. within our container. I'm dividing the container's width by. the path's width to get the horizontal. scale and the container's height by the. path height to get the vertical. scale. Then I'm taking the smaller one. to preserve the logo shape without. stretching it. The positioning. calculations help center our logo. I'm. starting with the container position,. then adding an offset to center the. logo, and finally adjusting for the. logo's internal coordinates. This ensures the logo appears exactly. where we want it in our. mask. Finally, I'm applying these. calculations to position and scale our. SVG path using the transform attribute. This tells the browser exactly where to. place our logo mask and how large it. should be. These precise calculations. are what allow our effect to work with.

any logo shape and size. Without them,. the mask wouldn't align properly and our. reveal effect wouldn't work correctly. Next, I'm creating our scroll trigger. instance. This is what will control all. of our effects as the user scrolls down. the page. I'll first set up the scroll. trigger with some important options. I'm. using hero section as the trigger. element, which means our animation will. start when this section enters the. viewport. I'm setting start to top. So,. the animation begins right away when the. top of our hero section reaches the top. of the viewport. For the end value, I'm. setting it to five times the viewport. height. This means our animation will. take place over a long scroll distance,. giving us plenty of room to create a. smooth gradual effect. I'm also enabling. pin, which keeps our hero section fixed. on screen while the animation plays, and. pin spacing, which maintains the scroll. height. The scrub value of one creates a. slight delay between scrolling and the. animation updating, making it feel. smoother. Next, I'm defining what. happens during the scroll in the. onupdate call back. This function. receives a self parameter that contains. information about the scroll progress. I'll start by getting the current scroll. progress which is a value between 0 and. 1 representing how far through the. animation we are. Then I'm calculating. the fade opacity for our initial. elements for the first 15% of scrolling. I want the logo and scroll text to. gradually fade out. I'm calculating this. with a formula that converts our. progress to an opacity value between 1. and zero. If this scroll progress is. below 15%, I'm setting the opacity of. these elements based on our calculation. Otherwise, I'm setting the opacity to. zero to keep them hidden. Next, for the. first 85% of scrolling, I'm handling the. scaling effects. First, I'm normalizing. the progress to a 01 range within this. portion of the scroll. I'm calculating. the scale of our hero image container,. starting at 1.5 and gradually reducing. to one as we scroll. This creates that. zoom out effect for the background. For. the overlay scale, I'm using a formula. that starts at our large initial overlay. scale value and gradually reduces it to. one. The power function helps create a.

smooth exponential transition instead of. a linear one. This is what creates the. effect of zooming out through the logo. shape. After 25% of scrolling, I start fading. in our white overlay. I'm calculating. its opacity to smoothly transition from. 0 to 1 between 25% and 65% of the. scroll. Finally, I'm applying these calculated. values using GAP's set method, which. updates the properties right away. Now,. for the final part of our scroll. animation, I'm adding the text reveal. effect that happens between 60% and 85%. of our scroll progress. When the scroll. progress is between 6 and 85, I want to. gradually reveal our heading text with a. smooth gradient effect. First, I'm. calculating a normalized progress value. just for this specific section of the. scroll. Converting it from a 0 to one. range. I'm setting a gradient spread of. 100, which determines how sharp or. gradual our text will be. Then, I'm. calculating the position of the. gradient's bottom edge, starting at 240. and moving upward as we scroll. The. gradient's top edge is positioned 100. pixels above the bottom edge. Then, I'm. scaling the text slightly as it appears,. starting from 1.25 25 times its normal. size and reducing it to one as you. scroll. This creates a subtle zoom. effect on the text itself. Now comes the. cool part. I am setting a linear. gradient as the background for our text. The gradient starts with a dark. background color at the top, then. transitions to a reddish color at the. bottom. The gradient positions are. dynamically calculated based on our. scroll progress. Because we set the text. to use background clip text in our CSS,. only the text itself shows this. gradient. As we scroll, the gradient. moves upward through the text, creating. that smooth rival effect from bottom to. top. I'm also gradually increasing the. opacity of the entire text element as we. scroll through this section. If the. scroll progress is less than 6, I'm. keeping the text completely invisible by. setting its opacity to zero. And with. that, our scroll trigger setup is. complete. This configuration creates the. entire animated sequence. Hope you found. the video helpful. See you in the next.

one.

=============

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lh5fQIlyOe0

Learn how to recreate GTA VI's mind-blowing scroll animation using HTML, CSS, JavaScript, GSAP and ScrollTrigger! This step-by-step tutorial shows you how to build that cinematic logo reveal effect that everyone's talking about.

Source Code: https://codegrid.gumroad.com/l/codegridpro/

Inspiration: Rockstar Games

Website: https://www.rockstargames.com/VI

Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/codegridweb/

Twitter: https://twitter.com/codegridweb/

Public Discord: https://discord.gg/B8B9MXxuSS

Music from Epidemic Sound.

Thanks for watching!
item.content:encoded:
影片摘要


影片的開頭，講者收到了許多觀眾的要求，希望示範如何重現GTA 6網站上令人驚豔的捲動動畫。他表示，這個效果非常吸引人，也因此會是個有趣的挑戰。這個動畫的特色是，當使用者向下捲動時，Logo和內容會平滑地消失，同時英雄圖片會向外縮放。接著，Logo會以類似挖空視窗的方式逐漸顯現，並透過其形狀揭示背景。再向下捲動，文字會以平滑的漸變效果出現。講者承認，重建這個效果比預期的複雜，經過七個小時的努力和四種不同的嘗試後，他成功地利用SVG、GSAP和Scroll Trigger重現了這個動畫效果。
他採用了SVG遮罩的方法來實現揭示效果。他建立了一個SVG遮罩，其中包含定義Logo形狀的路徑，然後將此遮罩應用到覆蓋視窗的矩形上，創造了一個Logo形狀的「窗口」，這個窗口會顯示下方的內容，並隱藏其他所有內容。影片將逐步示範如何重現相同的效果。
接著，講者提到了提供源碼的贊助方案，並鼓勵觀眾點讚和訂閱。

素材準備


首先，講者說明了專案所需的素材：英雄區塊的背景圖片、前景圖片（人物角色分離在透明背景上）、Logo的PNG版本、以及Logo的SVG版本。SVG版本尤其重要，需要從設計工具匯出，並提取路徑資料。為了方便操作，他建立了一個名為logo.js的獨立檔案，將這個路徑資料匯出為名為logoData的字串變數。這個logoData將被用於建立遮罩，透過Logo形狀來顯示背景。

HTML結構


接下來，講者概述了HTML結構。主要分為兩個部分：英雄區塊，包含動畫；以及在捲動後出現的後續區塊。
英雄區塊中，首先加入一個具有hero image container class的div，用於容納分層的圖片。在這個容器中，背景圖片作為第一個元素。接下來，加入一個具有hero image logo class的div，其中包含Logo圖片。這個Logo初始可見，但會在捲動時淡出。第三個元素是前景圖片，也就是分離的人物角色圖片。在這些圖片下方，增加一個具有hero image copy class的div，包含一段文字。
在圖片容器之外，增加一個fade overlay div，用於創建淡入效果。關鍵的元素是具有overlay class的div，其中包含SVG。在這個SVG中，定義了一個名為logo re mask的遮罩，包含一個全螢幕的矩形和一個具有ID為logo mask的路徑元素。這個路徑將使用JavaScript中的logoData填充。然後，將這個遮罩應用於填充深色的全螢幕矩形上，創造遮罩效果。
此外，還有一個空的logo container div，用於定位Logo遮罩。接著是包含標題文字的overlay copy div，文字會在捲動序列中稍後出現。最後，外部區塊將包含一些佔位符文字，在捲動完動畫後可見。

CSS樣式設定


接下來，講者開始樣式設定。首先，他導入了Google Fonts的DMsense字體。然後，重置所有預設的瀏覽器邊距和填充，並確保使用border box的盒模型。
接著，設定body的字體、深色背景和白色文字。他隱藏了水平溢出，以避免不必要的滾動條。他將所有圖片設定為佔據容器的100%寬度和高度，並使用object fit cover來保持長寬比。
對於排版，他將H1元素設定為大寫，使用大字體和緊密的行距，以及稍微負值的字母間距，營造現代感。段落也被設定為大寫，但字體較小且使用中等粗細的字重。
每個區塊都會佔據整個視窗的寬度和高度，並使用相對定位，以便對其中的元素進行絕對定位。背景顏色與body顏色相同，並且隱藏英雄圖片容器及其樣式圖片的溢出，以及fade overlay。他將它們進行絕對定位，以填充整個容器。
Logo圖片進行特殊處理，從頂部定位20%，並水平居中。他設定了特定的寬度，並允許高度自動調整，同時保持長寬比。捲動提示文字位於螢幕底部並居中。
fade overlay具有白色背景，會在捲動時逐漸改變不透明度。包含SVG遮罩的overlay被絕對定位，高度為200%，並使用Z軸索引1，確保其位於圖片之上。設定了transform origin為水平中心和垂直15%，用於控制縮放。
對於logo container，定位為從頂部算起25%，並水平居中，這決定了Logo遮罩出現的位置。他提到，這個值需要根據不同的Logo進行調整，並解釋了他的Logo中眼睛的設計。
包含文字的overlay copy位於視窗底部附近，並使用Z軸索引2，確保其出現在其他元素之上。對於此文字，使用了background clip技術來建立文字遮罩，以便稍後在JavaScript中顯示漸變背景。Transform origin設定為中心上方，控制動畫期間的縮放。
[音樂]
後續區塊的文字僅使用flex佈局水平和垂直居中。最後，針對較小的螢幕添加了一些響應式調整，縮小字體，並確保overlay copy在行動裝置上佔據全部寬度。

JavaScript程式碼


講者開始講解JavaScript，使頁面動起來。首先，他導入了必要的依賴項，包括logo.js中的SVG路徑資料、用於動畫的GSAP、用於觸發基於滾動位置動畫的GSAP的Scroll Trigger插件、以及用於平滑滾動體驗的Lenis。
導入後，他將Scroll Trigger插件註冊到GSAP，以便使用。然後，他將所有內容包裝在DOM content loaded事件監聽器中，確保程式碼在頁面完全載入後運行。
在事件監聽器中，他設定了Lenis平滑滾動，並讓Lenis在發生滾動時更新Scroll Trigger。他使用了現成的程式碼。
接著，他選取了所有DOM元素，包括背景圖片容器、起始Logo、捲動提示文字、白色overlay、包含遮罩的SVG overlay、以及標題文字。這些選取用於操作這些元素，使其隨著使用者捲動而改變。
接下來，他設定Logo遮罩及其尺寸。他定義了overlay的初始縮放值為350，這個數值決定了遮罩開始時的縮放程度，這個數值可以根據Logo類型進行調整。他選取了在HTML中定義的logo container，以及SVG中的logo mask元素。
接著是一個重要的步驟，他將SVG路徑的D屬性設定為使用匯入的logoData，這定義了SVG遮罩中Logo的實際形狀。
為了正確定位Logo，需要確定容器和SVG路徑的大小和位置。他使用get bounding client rect函式獲取容器的確切位置和尺寸，以及get bounding box方法獲取Logo形狀的尺寸。然後，他計算縮放因子。因為SVG Logo路徑需要正確地適合容器。他將容器的寬度除以路徑的寬度，得到水平縮放比例，並將容器的高度除以路徑的高度，得到垂直縮放比例。
================================================

In the last few days, a bunch of folks. reached out requesting a video on this. stunning scroll animation from the GTA 6. website. When I first saw it, even I was. instantly captivated and knew it would. be an exciting challenge to recreate. this effect from scratch. As you can. see, when scrolling down, the logo and. the content of this section smoothly. fade away while the hero image zooms. out. Then, the logo cinematically starts. coming into view as a cutout window. while still revealing the background. through its shape. As you scroll more,. some text emerges with a smooth gradient. fade. I'm not going to lie, when I. started trying to rebuild this, it. turned out way more complex than I. expected. But after about 7 hours and. trying out like four different. approaches, I was finally able to. replicate the scroll animation using. SVGs, GSAP, and scroll trigger. The. technique I landed on uses an SVG. masking approach for the reveal effect. Basically, I created an SVG mask with a. path inside it that defines the logo. shape and then applied this mask to a. rectangle covering the view port that. created sort of a window in the shape of. the logo that reveals what's underneath. while hiding everything else. In this. video, I'll show you step by step how to. recreate the same effect yourself. If. you find these videos helpful, a like. and subscribe would really mean a lot. Also, if you want to access the source. code for this project, plus hundreds of. other similar micro projects along with. the complete website template every. month, you can check out the pro. membership via the link in the. description. All right, let's dive in. Before we dive into coding, let's go. through the assets we'll need for this. project. First, we need a background. image for hero section. I'm using this. vibrant GTA themed artwork from 4K. wallpapers.com, but you can substitute. any image you prefer. Next, we need a. foreground image with just the. characters isolated on a transparent. background. This is crucial for our. layering effect. It allows us to place. the logo behind the characters, but in. front of the background. We'll stack. these elements with the background at. the bottom, logo in the middle, and.

character image on the top. Next, for. the logo, I have created the simple PNG. version in Figma. You can use any logo. design that works for your project. Most. importantly, you'll also need an SVG. version of your logo. After exporting. from your design tool, open the SVG in. any browser and extract the path data. from it. This path information is. essential for creating our mask effect. To make this easier, I've created a. separate file called logo.js that. exports this path data as a string. variable named logo data. We'll import. this into our main script and use it to. create the mask that reles our. background through the logo shape. With. these assets prepared, we are ready to. start building our HTML structure. We'll need two main sections. A hero. section which will contain our animation. and an outro section that appears after. scrolling. Inside the hero section, I'm. first adding a div with the class hero. image container that would hold our. layered images. Within this container,. I'm placing the background image as the. first element. This will be our main. backdrop. Next, I'm adding another div. with the class name hero image logo. which contains our logo image. This logo. will initially be visible but will fade. out as we scroll. Then I'm adding our. second layer image. This is isolated. character image that will appear on top. of everything else. Below that I'm. adding a div with the class hero image. copy containing a paragraph text. Now. outside the image container I'm adding a. fade overlay div which will help create. a fade effect as we scroll. The next. element is crucial a div with the class. name overlay containing an SVG. This is. where the magic happens. Inside the SVG,. I have defined a mask called logo re. mask which contains a full screen. rectangle and a path element with the ID. logo mask. This path will be populated. with our logo data from JavaScript. Then. I'm applying this mask to a full screen. rectangle filled with a dark color. This. creates our mask effect where the logo. shape will reveal the content. underneath. I'm also adding an empty. logo container div which will be used as. a reference point for positioning our. logo mask. Then there is an overlay copy. div containing an H1 with a text that.

will appear later in the scroll. sequence. Finally, the outer section. will have some placeholder text that. will be visible after scrolling past the. animation. That would be it for the HTML. structure. Let's get to styling. now. First, you can see I've imported. the DMsense font from Google Fonts. Now. let's start by resetting all default. browser margins, paddings, and ensuring. box sizing is set to border box. For the. body, I'm setting our font family to. DMs, giving it a dark background color,. and white text. I'm also hiding any. horizontal overflow to prevent unwanted. scroll bars. I'm setting all images to. take up 100% of their containers width. and height with object fit cover to. maintain aspect ratios. For typography,. I'm styling the H1 elements to be. uppercase, giving a large font size with. tight line spacing and slightly negative. letter spacing for that modern. look. Paragraphs are also styled to be. uppercase but smaller and with medium. font. weight. Each section will take up the. full view port width and height with. relative positioning. So, we can. absolutely position elements inside. them. I'm setting the background to. match our body color and hiding any. overflow for our hero image container. and its styled images as well as the. fade overlay. I'm absolutely positioning. them to fill the entire. container. The logo image gets special. treatment. It's positioned 20% from the. top and centered horizontally. I am setting a specific width and. allowing the height to adjust. automatically while maintaining the. aspect. ratio. The scroll prompt text is. positioned at the bottom of the screen. and. centered. I'm adding will change opacity. to optimize the animation. performance. Our fade overlay is given a white. background and will gradually change. opacity during. scrolling. The overlay that contains our. SVG mask is positioned absolutely but. with 200% height and a Z index of one to. ensure it sits above our images. I am. setting its transform origin to the. center horizontally and 15% vertically.

which will control how it scales. Now. for the logo container, this is crucial. I'm positioning it fixed at 25% from the. top and centered horizontally. This. position is important because it. determines where your logo mask will. appear. You'll likely need to adjust. this value based on your specific logo. In my case, I wanted the zoom effect to. happen through the eye character present. in the last line in my logo. So, 25%. worked well. Different logos will. require different positioning. So, be. prepared to experiment with this value. The overlay copy that contains our text. is positioned near the bottom of the. viewport with a zindex of two to ensure. it appears above the other. elements. For this text, I'm using the. background clip technique to create a. text mask that will reveal a gradient. background later in our. JavaScript. I'm setting the transform. origin to the center top which will. control how it scales during animation. [Music]. The outro section text is simply. centered horizontally and vertically. using flex. layout. Finally, I'm adding some. responsive adjustments for smaller. screens, reducing font sizes, and. ensuring the overlay copy takes the full. width on mobile. devices. Let's get to JavaScript now and. bring this page to life. First, I'm importing the necessary. dependencies. The logo data from our. logojs file which contains the SVG path. data. Gap for handling our animations. Scroll trigger plug-in for GSAP which. will trigger animations based on scroll. position. Lenis, a smooth scrolling. library to make the experience more. polished. After importing these, I'm. registering the scroll trigger plug-in. with GSA so we can use it. Now I'm. wrapping everything in a DOM content. loaded event listener to make sure our. code only runs after the page has fully. loaded. Inside this event listener I'm. setting up Lennis source mode scrolling. will create a new Lennis instance and. tells it to update scroll trigger. whenever scrolling happens. You can find. this block of code on the documentation. website. I've used it as is without any. change.

Now I'm selecting all DOM elements. We'll need to animate the container with. our background images, the logo that. appears at the beginning, the scroll. down text, the white overlay that will. fade in, the SVG overlay that contains. our mask, the heading text that will. appear later in the animation. These. selections give us references to. manipulate these elements as the user. scrolls. Next, I'm setting up the logo. mask and its dimensions. I start by. defining an initial scale value of 350. for our overlay. This is a really large. number that determines how zoomed in the. mask will be at the beginning. You can. play around this value based on your. logo type. I am selecting the logo. container we defined in our HTML and the. logo mask element from our SVG. Now. comes an important step. I'm setting the. D attribute of our SVG path to use the. logo data we imported. This is what. defines the actual shape of our logo in. the SVG mask. To position the logo. properly, I need to figure out both the. size and the position of our container. and the SVG path. For the container, I'm. using get bounding client rect function. which gives me its exact position and. dimensions on the page. For the SVG. path, I'm using get bounding box method. which tells me the dimensions of our. logo shape. Now I'm calculating scaling. factors. This is important because our. SVG logo path needs to properly fit. within our container. I'm dividing the container's width by. the path's width to get the horizontal. scale and the container's height by the. path height to get the vertical. scale. Then I'm taking the smaller one. to preserve the logo shape without. stretching it. The positioning. calculations help center our logo. I'm. starting with the container position,. then adding an offset to center the. logo, and finally adjusting for the. logo's internal coordinates. This ensures the logo appears exactly. where we want it in our. mask. Finally, I'm applying these. calculations to position and scale our. SVG path using the transform attribute. This tells the browser exactly where to. place our logo mask and how large it. should be. These precise calculations. are what allow our effect to work with.

any logo shape and size. Without them,. the mask wouldn't align properly and our. reveal effect wouldn't work correctly. Next, I'm creating our scroll trigger. instance. This is what will control all. of our effects as the user scrolls down. the page. I'll first set up the scroll. trigger with some important options. I'm. using hero section as the trigger. element, which means our animation will. start when this section enters the. viewport. I'm setting start to top. So,. the animation begins right away when the. top of our hero section reaches the top. of the viewport. For the end value, I'm. setting it to five times the viewport. height. This means our animation will. take place over a long scroll distance,. giving us plenty of room to create a. smooth gradual effect. I'm also enabling. pin, which keeps our hero section fixed. on screen while the animation plays, and. pin spacing, which maintains the scroll. height. The scrub value of one creates a. slight delay between scrolling and the. animation updating, making it feel. smoother. Next, I'm defining what. happens during the scroll in the. onupdate call back. This function. receives a self parameter that contains. information about the scroll progress. I'll start by getting the current scroll. progress which is a value between 0 and. 1 representing how far through the. animation we are. Then I'm calculating. the fade opacity for our initial. elements for the first 15% of scrolling. I want the logo and scroll text to. gradually fade out. I'm calculating this. with a formula that converts our. progress to an opacity value between 1. and zero. If this scroll progress is. below 15%, I'm setting the opacity of. these elements based on our calculation. Otherwise, I'm setting the opacity to. zero to keep them hidden. Next, for the. first 85% of scrolling, I'm handling the. scaling effects. First, I'm normalizing. the progress to a 01 range within this. portion of the scroll. I'm calculating. the scale of our hero image container,. starting at 1.5 and gradually reducing. to one as we scroll. This creates that. zoom out effect for the background. For. the overlay scale, I'm using a formula. that starts at our large initial overlay. scale value and gradually reduces it to. one. The power function helps create a.

smooth exponential transition instead of. a linear one. This is what creates the. effect of zooming out through the logo. shape. After 25% of scrolling, I start fading. in our white overlay. I'm calculating. its opacity to smoothly transition from. 0 to 1 between 25% and 65% of the. scroll. Finally, I'm applying these calculated. values using GAP's set method, which. updates the properties right away. Now,. for the final part of our scroll. animation, I'm adding the text reveal. effect that happens between 60% and 85%. of our scroll progress. When the scroll. progress is between 6 and 85, I want to. gradually reveal our heading text with a. smooth gradient effect. First, I'm. calculating a normalized progress value. just for this specific section of the. scroll. Converting it from a 0 to one. range. I'm setting a gradient spread of. 100, which determines how sharp or. gradual our text will be. Then, I'm. calculating the position of the. gradient's bottom edge, starting at 240. and moving upward as we scroll. The. gradient's top edge is positioned 100. pixels above the bottom edge. Then, I'm. scaling the text slightly as it appears,. starting from 1.25 25 times its normal. size and reducing it to one as you. scroll. This creates a subtle zoom. effect on the text itself. Now comes the. cool part. I am setting a linear. gradient as the background for our text. The gradient starts with a dark. background color at the top, then. transitions to a reddish color at the. bottom. The gradient positions are. dynamically calculated based on our. scroll progress. Because we set the text. to use background clip text in our CSS,. only the text itself shows this. gradient. As we scroll, the gradient. moves upward through the text, creating. that smooth rival effect from bottom to. top. I'm also gradually increasing the. opacity of the entire text element as we. scroll through this section. If the. scroll progress is less than 6, I'm. keeping the text completely invisible by. setting its opacity to zero. And with. that, our scroll trigger setup is. complete. This configuration creates the. entire animated sequence. Hope you found. the video helpful. See you in the next.

one.

=============

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lh5fQIlyOe0

Learn how to recreate GTA VI's mind-blowing scroll animation using HTML, CSS, JavaScript, GSAP and ScrollTrigger! This step-by-step tutorial shows you how to build that cinematic logo reveal effect that everyone's talking about.

Source Code: https://codegrid.gumroad.com/l/codegridpro/

Inspiration: Rockstar Games

Website: https://www.rockstargames.com/VI

Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/codegridweb/

Twitter: https://twitter.com/codegridweb/

Public Discord: https://discord.gg/B8B9MXxuSS

Music from Epidemic Sound.

Thanks for watching!

item.title: This GSAP Hover Animation Makes Visitors OBSESS Over Your Team Section
item.itunes:title: This GSAP Hover Animation Makes Visitors OBSESS Over Your Team Section
item.itunes:author: Codegrid
item.itunes:summary:
靈感來源


影片的作者分享了他在瀏覽網站設計時，發現了一個令人印象深刻的微互動。這是一個網站去年八月獲得「每日網站」殊榮的作品，網站充滿了令人驚豔的動畫。其中，作者特別喜歡團隊成員區塊的滑鼠懸停效果。當滑鼠停留在個人檔案圖片上時，圖片會平滑放大，同時展現出相應的姓名，並伴隨著酷炫的交錯文字動畫。滑鼠移開後，一切又會優雅地恢復原狀。

建立互動效果


作者決定重現這個效果，包括動畫、圖片排列和文字效果，使用的技術包含 HTML、CSS、JavaScript、GSAP 函式庫，以及 GSAP 的 split text 外掛程式。他還提到了為 Pro 會員建立了一個 NexJS 版本。作者將逐步示範如何從頭開始建立這個互動元素。

HTML 結構


影片首先建立了 HTML 結構。他使用了一個帶有 "team" 類別的 section。這個 section 分為兩個主要的容器：用於圖片的 "profile images" 和用於文字的 "profile names"。在 "profile images" 中，建立了九個帶有 "image" 類別的 div，每個 div 包含一個 image 元素。在 "profile names" 中，添加了十個帶有 "name" 類別的 div，其中第一個 div 帶有 "default" 類別。 "default" 類別的文字會從上方滑入，而其他名字會在對應的圖片被懸停時，從下方動畫向上。

CSS 設定


接下來，作者展示了 CSS 的設定。他導入了 Barlo Condensed 字體，並設置了基本的 reset，將所有元素的 margin 和 padding 設為零，並使用 box-sizing: border-box。圖片設置為完全響應式，使用 object-fit: cover 維持長寬比。 "team" 區塊設定為視窗全螢幕寬度和高度，背景色為深色，文字為白色，使用 flexbox 居中排列。 他還添加了容器之間的間距。 "profile images" 容器使用 flexbox，寬度設定為 max-content。每個 "image" div 設定為 70x70 像素，帶有邊框半徑和 pointer cursor。"profile names" 容器設置了固定高度，並使用 clip-path 創建矩形剪裁區域，確保文字動畫只在區域內顯示。 "h1" 元素使用 absolute 定位、全寬、居中對齊，並使用自定義字體，字體大小很大。文字設定為大寫，字母間距緊湊，顏色為紅色。初始狀態下，它們定位於螢幕外，translate-Y 為 100%。預設文字 "default" 則被設定為不同的顏色，定位在容器上方，translate-Y 為 -100%。每個 "h1" 內的字母都被設定為相對定位，為交錯動畫做準備。最後，添加了媒體查詢，在螢幕小於 900 像素時，更改 flex 方向，將圖片換行，縮小尺寸，並調整字體大小以改善手機體驗。

JavaScript 程式碼


在 JavaScript 部分，首先導入了 GSAP 函式庫和 split text 外掛程式，並註冊外掛程式。然後，將所有內容包裹在 DOM content loaded 事件監聽器中，確保 HTML 加載完成。接下來，作者選取了所有需要的元素：圖片容器、個別圖片 div、名稱元素和 h1 標題。 他使用 split text 將文字分割成單個字元，以便進行動畫製作，並將 "letter" 類別添加到每個字元。對於初始狀態，預設文字的字母 Y 位置被設定為 100%。 在添加滑鼠事件之前，作者檢查了螢幕寬度是否大於 900 像素，以確保滑鼠懸停動畫僅在桌面裝置上運行。作者循環遍歷每個圖片，為其設定懸停交互。當滑鼠進入時，會觸發兩個動畫。 首先，圖片會放大到 140 像素，耗時 0.5 秒，使用 power out easing。 其次，對應的姓名字母向上動畫，translate-Y 為 -100%，耗時 0.75 秒，並包括交錯效果。當滑鼠移開時，動畫反向進行。 最後，為整個圖片容器添加了懸停互動。當滑鼠進入容器時，預設文字的字母從 Y 100% 動畫到 0%，滑入視窗。當滑鼠移出容器時，預設文字字母又會滑出視窗。
================================================

Last week, while scrolling through. awards collections for inspiration, I. discovered this incredible micro. interaction that right away caught my. attention. This was from a website that. actually won site of the day back in. August last year. And let me tell you,. when you explore the full site, you will. see it's packed with impressive. animations throughout. What really stood. out to me in the robots showcase was. this brilliant hover effect they used. for their team section. Watch this. As. you hover over any profile image, it. smoothly scales up while simultaneously. revealing the corresponding name with a. cool staggered text animation. Move your. cursor away and everything elegantly. transitions back to the default state. I. thought this would be an interesting. challenge to recreate. So, I tried to. build this exact same effect including. both animations, the images dock, and. the text re all using HTML, CSS,. JavaScript, GSAP, and GSA's split text. plug-in. For those interested, I also. created a NexJS version that's available. exclusively for pro members. Since Gap. plugins are now free, it's amazing how. easy the process has become to create. such micro animations. In this video,. I'll show you exactly how to create this. interactive element from scratch, step.

by step. Please make sure to leave a. like on the video and subscribe if you. haven't already. Also, if you'd like to. access the source code for this project. and hundreds of other micro projects. along with a complete website template. every month, you can check out the pro. membership via the link in the. description. All right, let's dive into. the. code. For this project, we need just one. section with the class team. I'll split. this into two main containers. Profile. images for our images dock and profile. names to display the. text. Inside profile images, I'll create. nine divs with the class image, each. containing an image element. These form. our row of profile pictures at the top. For the name section, I'll add 10 divs. with the class name. Notice we have one. extra div here that will display our. default text when no image is hovered. We'll use H1 elements to render the. names. Make sure to add an extra class. name called default to the first one as. it will be positioned differently. The. default text slides in from above while. all other names animate up from below. when their corresponding image is. hovered. That covers our HTML structure. Now let's move on to the. CSS. First you can see I've imported the.

barlo condensed font from Google fonts. which we'll use for our large text. display. Next I'll set up our basic. reset with margin and padding set to. zero and box sizing to border box for. all elements. For images, I'll make them. fully responsive with width and height. at 100% and object fit cover to maintain. aspect. ratio. Now for our team section, I'll. make it full view portut width and. height. I'll set a dark background. color, white text, and use flexbox to. center everything with a column. direction. [Music]. I'll also add some gap between both the. containers. For the profile images. container, I'll use display flex with. max content width so it only takes up. the space it. needs. Each image div will be 70 by 70. pixels with 5 pixels of padding and a. pointer cursor. The images themselves will have a subtle. border radius for rounded. corners. Now for the profile names. container. This is where it gets. interesting. I'll set a fixed height for. this drapper and use clip path to create. a rectangular clipping area. This. ensures our text animations only appear. within bounds.

For the H1 elements, I'll position them. absolutely, make them full width,. centered, aligned, and use our custom. font at a massive font size. Same as the. container. side, I'll set them to uppercase with. tight letter spacing and a red color. Initially they are positioned off screen. but translate by. 100%. The default text gets special. treatment. It's colored differently and. positioned above the container with. translate Y minus. 100%. Each letter within the H1 will be. positioned relatively ready for our. staggered animation. Finally, I'll add a media query for. screens under 900 pixels on mobile. I'll. reverse the flex direction, wrap the. images into rows, reduce sizes, and. adjust the font size for better mobile. experience. That's our complete CSS setup, creating. the foundation for our interactive. animation. Now, let's dive into the. JavaScript where all the magic. happens. First, I'll import GAP and the. split text. [Music]. plugin. Then, register the plugin with. GAP so we can use it. Next, we'll wrap everything in a DOM. content loaded event listener to ensure.

our HTML is fully loaded before we start. manipulating it. Now, let's start by. selecting all the elements we need. The. profile images container, all the. individual image divs, all the name. elements, all the H1 headings inside the. names. Next, I'll split our text into. individual characters for animation. I'll loop through each name heading and. use split text to break it into. characters. This allows us to create that staggered. animation where each letter moves. independently. After splitting, I'll add. a letter class to each. character. This helps us target these. elements for. animation. Now, for the initial state,. I'll select the letters in our default. text and set their Y position to 100%. This might seem odd since our CSS. positions the default H1 at minus 100%. But we are positioning the letters. within their already elevated parent. This setup allows the letters to slide. down into view when animated. Now before. adding any mouse events, I'll check if. the screen width is 900 pixels or. greater. This ensures our hover. animations only work on desktop devices,. preventing unwanted behavior on. touchscreens where hover doesn't make. sense. Now I loop through each profile.

image to set up their individual hover. interactions. For each image, I need to. find its corresponding name element. This is where our HTML structure becomes. important. Since our default text is at. index zero, I'll use index + one to. match each image with the correct name. So image zero corresponds to name one,. image one to name two, and so on. I'll. then select all the letter elements. within the specific name div, which will. animate when this particular image is. forwarded. On mouse enter, I'll trigger. two animations simultaneously. First,. the image itself scales up from 70. pixels to 140 pixels, exactly double its. original size. The scaling happens. over.5 seconds with a powerful out. easing, creating a smooth expansion. effect. Second, the corresponding name. letters animate upward with translate Y. minus 100%. This moves them from their. hidden position below the container into. the visible area. The letter animation. is slightly longer at 75 seconds and. includes a signature stagger effect. Each letter moves with a 025 second. delay starting from center and rippling. outward. On mouse leave, everything. reverses. The image smoothly shrinks. back to its original size 70 pixels. The letters slide back down to their. starting position at Y0% disappearing.

from. view. Both animations maintain the same. timing and easing for consistency,. creating a polished. interaction. Finally, I'll add hover. interactions for the entire profile. images container. This handles the. animation of our default text, the. squad. When the mouse enters anywhere in. the container, I'll animate the default. letters from their hidden position at Y. 100% to. 0%. This brings them down into view with. the same staggered animation we used for. the. names. When the mouse leaves the. container completely, the default. letters animate back to 100% sliding out. of view again. These container level. animations ensure that whenever you are. hovering over the image grid area, you. see the default text, but it gracefully. exits when you move away from the entire. section. The timing and easing match our. other animations 75 seconds with power. for out easing and a 0 to 5 seconds. staggered from the center. This. consistency is key to making the entire. interaction feel cohesive and polished. And that completes our JavaScript. We. have created a fully interactive team. section where images scale on hover. names animate with beautiful staggered.

effects and the default text response to. the container interactions. Hope you. found the video helpful. See you in the. next one.

=============

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yCYbH0K5Dh0

Learn how to create a mesmerizing team section with staggered text animations that react to cursor movement, using HTML, CSS, JavaScript, GSAP, and SplitText plugin — transform ordinary profile pictures into interactive experiences that captivate visitors!

Source Code: https://codegrid.gumroad.com/l/codegridpro/

Inspiration: O Positive Films

Website: https://opos.buzzworthystudio.com/

Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/codegridweb/

Twitter: https://twitter.com/codegridweb/

Public Discord: https://discord.gg/B8B9MXxuSS

Music from Epidemic Sound.

Thanks for watching!
item.description:
靈感來源


影片的作者分享了他在瀏覽網站設計時，發現了一個令人印象深刻的微互動。這是一個網站去年八月獲得「每日網站」殊榮的作品，網站充滿了令人驚豔的動畫。其中，作者特別喜歡團隊成員區塊的滑鼠懸停效果。當滑鼠停留在個人檔案圖片上時，圖片會平滑放大，同時展現出相應的姓名，並伴隨著酷炫的交錯文字動畫。滑鼠移開後，一切又會優雅地恢復原狀。
